title: Drainage basin

infobox.value: is an area of land in which all flowing [surface water](/wiki/Surface_water "Surface water") converges to a single point, such as a [river mouth](/wiki/River_mouth "River mouth"), or flows into another [body of water](/wiki/Body_of_water "Body of water"), such as a [lake](/wiki/Lake "Lake") or [ocean](/wiki/Ocean "Ocean"). A basin is separated from adjacent basins by a perimeter, the [drainage divide](/wiki/Drainage_divide "Drainage divide"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) made up of a succession of elevated features, such as [ridges](/wiki/Ridge "Ridge") and [hills](/wiki/Hill "Hill"). A basin may consist of smaller basins that merge at river [confluences](/wiki/Confluence "Confluence"), forming a [hierarchical pattern](/wiki/Strahler_number "Strahler number").[[2]](#cite_note-whatis-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Land area where water converges to a common outlet

"Catchment" and "Catchment basin" redirect here. For the human geography concept, see [Catchment area](/wiki/Catchment_area "Catchment area").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Major_River_Systems_within_the_Mississippi_River_Basin.svg/500px-Major_River_Systems_within_the_Mississippi_River_Basin.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Major_River_Systems_within_the_Mississippi_River_Basin.svg)

The [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") drains the largest area of any [U.S.](/wiki/United_States "United States") river, much of it [agricultural](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") regions. Agricultural runoff and other water pollution that flows to the outlet is the cause of the [hypoxic or dead zone](/wiki/Dead_zone_(ecology) "Dead zone (ecology)") in the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico").

A **drainage basin** is an area of land in which all flowing [surface water](/wiki/Surface_water "Surface water") converges to a single point, such as a [river mouth](/wiki/River_mouth "River mouth"), or flows into another [body of water](/wiki/Body_of_water "Body of water"), such as a [lake](/wiki/Lake "Lake") or [ocean](/wiki/Ocean "Ocean"). A basin is separated from adjacent basins by a perimeter, the [drainage divide](/wiki/Drainage_divide "Drainage divide"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) made up of a succession of elevated features, such as [ridges](/wiki/Ridge "Ridge") and [hills](/wiki/Hill "Hill"). A basin may consist of smaller basins that merge at river [confluences](/wiki/Confluence "Confluence"), forming a [hierarchical pattern](/wiki/Strahler_number "Strahler number").[[2]](#cite_note-whatis-2)

Other terms for a drainage basin are **catchment area**, **catchment basin**, **drainage area**, **river basin**, **water catchment**, **water basin**,[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) and [impluvium](/wiki/Impluvium "Impluvium").[[5]](#cite_note-Huneau-2013-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) In North America, they are commonly called a **watershed**, though in other English-speaking places, "watershed" is used only in its original sense, that of the [drainage divide](/wiki/Drainage_divide "Drainage divide") line.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A drainage basin's boundaries are determined by [watershed delineation](/wiki/Watershed_delineation "Watershed delineation"), a common task in environmental engineering and science.

In a closed drainage basin, or [endorheic basin](/wiki/Endorheic_basin "Endorheic basin"), rather than flowing to the ocean, water converges toward the interior of the basin, known as a [sink](/wiki/Sink_(geography) "Sink (geography)"), which may be a permanent lake, a [dry lake](/wiki/Dry_lake "Dry lake"), or a point where surface water is [lost underground](/wiki/Losing_stream "Losing stream").[[8]](#cite_note-virginia-8)

Drainage basins are similar but not identical to [hydrologic units](/wiki/Hydrologic_unit "Hydrologic unit"), which are drainage areas delineated so as to nest into a multi-level hierarchical [drainage system](/wiki/Drainage_system_(geomorphology) "Drainage system (geomorphology)"). Hydrologic units are defined to allow multiple inlets, outlets, or sinks. In a strict sense, all drainage basins are hydrologic units, but not all hydrologic units are drainage basins.[[8]](#cite_note-virginia-8)

## Major drainage basins of the world

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Major drainage basins of the world")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of drainage basins by area](/wiki/List_of_drainage_basins_by_area "List of drainage basins by area").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Ocean_drainage.png/960px-Ocean_drainage.png)](/wiki/File:Ocean_drainage.png)

Major [continental divides](/wiki/Continental_divide "Continental divide"), showing how terrestrial drainage basins drain into the oceans. Grey areas are [endorheic basins](/wiki/Endorheic_basin "Endorheic basin") that do not drain to the oceans

### Ocean basins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ocean basins")]

About 48.71% of the world's land drains to the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), surface water drains to the Atlantic via the [Saint Lawrence River](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_River "Saint Lawrence River") and [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") basins, the [Eastern Seaboard](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") of the United States, the [Canadian Maritimes](/wiki/Maritimes "Maritimes"), and most of [Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador"). Nearly all of [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America") east of the [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes") also drains to the Atlantic, as does most of [Western](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") and [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and the greatest portion of western [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"), as well as [Western Sahara](/wiki/Western_Sahara "Western Sahara") and part of [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco").

The two major [Mediterranean seas](/wiki/Mediterranean_seas "Mediterranean seas") of the world also flow to the Atlantic. The [Caribbean Sea](/wiki/Caribbean_Sea "Caribbean Sea") and [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico") basin includes most of the U.S. interior between the [Appalachian](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains") and [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains"), a small part of the Canadian provinces of [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") and [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan"), eastern [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America"), the islands of the Caribbean and the Gulf, and a small part of northern South America. The [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") basin, with the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"), includes much of [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"), east-central [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") (through the [Nile River](/wiki/Nile_River "Nile River")), [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe"), Central, and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), and the coastal areas of [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon"), and [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria").

The [Arctic Ocean](/wiki/Arctic_Ocean "Arctic Ocean") drains most of [Western Canada](/wiki/Western_Canada "Western Canada") and [Northern Canada](/wiki/Northern_Canada "Northern Canada") east of the [Continental Divide](/wiki/Continental_Divide_of_the_Americas "Continental Divide of the Americas"), northern [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") and parts of [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota"), [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"), [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"), and [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana") in the United States, the north shore of the [Scandinavian Peninsula](/wiki/Scandinavian_Peninsula "Scandinavian Peninsula") in Europe, central and northern Russia, and parts of [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan") and [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia") in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), which totals to about 17% of the world's land.[[9]](#cite_note-Vörösmarty-2000-9)

Just over 13% of the land in the world drains to the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean").[[9]](#cite_note-Vörösmarty-2000-9) Its basin includes much of China, eastern and southeastern Russia, Japan, the [Korean Peninsula](/wiki/Korean_Peninsula "Korean Peninsula"), most of Indochina, Indonesia and Malaysia, the Philippines, all of the [Pacific Islands](/wiki/Pacific_Islands "Pacific Islands"), the northeast coast of [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), and Canada and the United States west of the Continental Divide (including most of Alaska), as well as western Central America and South America west of the Andes.

The [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean")'s drainage basin also comprises about 13% of Earth's land. It drains the eastern coast of Africa, the coasts of the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea") and the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf"), the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), Burma, and most parts of [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Largest river basins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Largest river basins")]

The five largest river basins (by area), from largest to smallest, are those of the [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_River "Amazon River") (7 million km2), the [Congo](/wiki/Congo_River "Congo River") (4 million km2), the [Nile](/wiki/Nile_River "Nile River") (3.4 million km2), the [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") (3.22 million km2), and the [Río de la Plata](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "Río de la Plata") (3.17 million km2). The three rivers that drain the most water, from most to least, are the Amazon, [Ganges](/wiki/Ganges "Ganges"), and Congo rivers.

### Endorheic drainage basins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Endorheic drainage basins")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Uureg_Nuur.jpg/250px-Uureg_Nuur.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uureg_Nuur.jpg)

[Endorheic basin](/wiki/Endorheic_basin "Endorheic basin") in [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia")

Main article: [Endorheic basin](/wiki/Endorheic_basin "Endorheic basin")

[Endorheic basin](/wiki/Endorheic_basin "Endorheic basin") are inland basins that do not drain to an ocean. Endorheic basins cover around 18% of the Earth's land. Some endorheic basins drain to an [Endorheic lake](/wiki/Endorheic_lake "Endorheic lake") or [Inland sea](/wiki/Inland_sea "Inland sea"). Many of these lakes are [ephemeral](/wiki/Ephemeral "Ephemeral") or vary dramatically in size depending on climate and inflow. If water evaporates or infiltrates into the ground at its terminus, the area can go by several names, such playa, salt flat, [dry lake](/wiki/Dry_lake "Dry lake"), or [alkali sink](/wiki/Alkali_sink "Alkali sink").

The largest endorheic basins are in Central [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), including the [Caspian Sea](/wiki/Caspian_Sea "Caspian Sea"), the [Aral Sea](/wiki/Aral_Sea "Aral Sea"), and numerous smaller lakes. Other endorheic regions include the [Great Basin](/wiki/Great_Basin "Great Basin") in the United States, much of the [Sahara Desert](/wiki/Sahara_Desert "Sahara Desert"), the drainage basin of the [Okavango River](/wiki/Okavango_River "Okavango River") ([Kalahari Basin](/wiki/Kalahari_Basin "Kalahari Basin")), highlands near the [African Great Lakes](/wiki/African_Great_Lakes "African Great Lakes"), the interiors of [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and the [Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula"), and parts in [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") and the [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes"). Some of these, such as the Great Basin, are not single drainage basins but collections of separate, adjacent closed basins.

In endorheic [bodies of water](/wiki/Bodies_of_water "Bodies of water") where evaporation is the primary means of water loss, the water is typically more saline than the oceans. An extreme example of this is the [Dead Sea](/wiki/Dead_Sea "Dead Sea").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Importance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Importance")]

### Geopolitical boundaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geopolitical boundaries")]

Drainage basins have been historically important for determining territorial boundaries, particularly in regions where trade by water has been important. For example, the [English](/wiki/England "England") crown gave the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") a monopoly on the [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") in the entire [Hudson Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay") basin, an area called [Rupert's Land](/wiki/Rupert%27s_Land "Rupert's Land"). [Bioregional](/wiki/Bioregionalism "Bioregionalism") political organization today includes agreements of states (e.g., international [treaties](/wiki/Treaty "Treaty") and, within the US, [interstate compacts](/wiki/Interstate_compact "Interstate compact")) or other political entities in a particular drainage basin to manage the body or bodies of water into which it drains. Examples of such interstate compacts are the [Great Lakes Commission](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Commission "Great Lakes Commission") and the [Tahoe Regional Planning Agency](/wiki/Tahoe_Regional_Planning_Agency "Tahoe Regional Planning Agency").

### Hydrology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Hydrology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Ohiorivermap.png/250px-Ohiorivermap.png)](/wiki/File:Ohiorivermap.png)

Drainage basin of the [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River"), part of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") drainage basin

In [hydrology](/wiki/Hydrology "Hydrology"), the drainage basin is a logical unit of focus[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] for studying the movement of water within the [hydrological cycle](/wiki/Hydrological_cycle "Hydrological cycle"). The process of finding a drainage boundary is referred to as [watershed delineation](/wiki/Watershed_delineation "Watershed delineation"). Finding the area and extent of a drainage basin is an important step in many areas of science and engineering.

Most of the water that discharges from the basin outlet originated as [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") falling on the basin.[[11]](#cite_note-11) A portion of the water that enters the [groundwater](/wiki/Groundwater "Groundwater") system beneath the drainage basin may flow towards the outlet of another drainage basin because groundwater flow directions do not always match those of their overlying drainage network. Measurement of the discharge of water from a basin may be made by a [stream gauge](/wiki/Stream_gauge "Stream gauge") located at the basin's outlet. Depending on the conditions of the drainage basin, as rainfall occurs some of it seeps directly into the ground. This water will either remain underground, slowly making its way downhill and eventually reaching the basin, or it will permeate deeper into the soil and consolidate into groundwater aquifers.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

As water flows through the basin, it can form tributaries that change the structure of the land. There are three different main types, which are affected by the rocks and ground underneath. Rock that is quick to erode forms [dendritic](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/dendritic "wiktionary:dendritic") patterns, and these are seen most often. The two other types of patterns that form are trellis patterns and rectangular patterns.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Rain gauge data is used to measure total precipitation over a drainage basin, and there are different ways to interpret that data. In the unlikely event that the gauges are many and evenly distributed over an area of uniform precipitation, using the [arithmetic mean](/wiki/Arithmetic_mean "Arithmetic mean") method will give good results. In the [Thiessen polygon](/wiki/Thiessen_polygon "Thiessen polygon") method, the drainage basin is divided into polygons with the rain gauge in the middle of each polygon assumed to be representative for the rainfall on the area of land included in its polygon. These polygons are made by drawing lines between gauges, then making perpendicular bisectors of those lines form the polygons. The [isohyetal](/wiki/Isohyet "Isohyet") method involves contours of equal precipitation are drawn over the gauges on a map. Calculating the area between these curves and adding up the volume of water is time-consuming.

[Isochrone maps](/wiki/Isochrone_map "Isochrone map") can be used to show the time taken for [runoff water](/wiki/Runoff_water "Runoff water") within a drainage basin to reach a lake, reservoir or outlet, assuming constant and uniform effective rainfall.[[14]](#cite_note-hydrolearthsystsci_bell_1998-14)[[15]](#cite_note-subramanya_2008-15)[[16]](#cite_note-unesco-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Geomorphology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geomorphology")]

Drainage basins are the principal hydrologic unit considered in [fluvial](/wiki/Fluvial "Fluvial") [geomorphology](/wiki/Geomorphology "Geomorphology"). A drainage basin is the source for water and [sediment](/wiki/Sediment "Sediment") that moves from higher elevation through the river system to lower elevations as they reshape the channel forms.

### Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ecology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Hydrographic_basin.svg/500px-Hydrographic_basin.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Hydrographic_basin.svg)

Top-down illustration of a [dendritic](/wiki/Drainage_system_(geomorphology) "Drainage system (geomorphology)") drainage basin. The dashed line is the main water divide of the [hydrography](/wiki/Hydrography "Hydrography") basin.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/EN_Bazinul_hidrografic_al_Raului_Latorita%2C_Romania.jpg/500px-EN_Bazinul_hidrografic_al_Raului_Latorita%2C_Romania.jpg)](/wiki/File:EN_Bazinul_hidrografic_al_Raului_Latorita,_Romania.jpg)

Digital [terrain map](/wiki/Relief_mapping_(computer_graphics) "Relief mapping (computer graphics)") of the [Latorița River](/wiki/Latori%C8%9Ba_River "Latorița River")'s drainage basin in [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania")

Drainage basins are important in [ecology](/wiki/Ecology "Ecology"). As water flows over the ground and along rivers it can pick up [nutrients](/wiki/Nutrient "Nutrient"), [sediment](/wiki/Sediment "Sediment"), and [pollutants](/wiki/Water_pollution "Water pollution"). With the water, they are transported towards the outlet of the basin, and can affect the ecological processes along the way as well as in the receiving [water body](/wiki/Water_body "Water body").

Modern use of [artificial fertilizers](/wiki/Artificial_fertilizer "Artificial fertilizer"), containing [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") (as [nitrates](/wiki/Nitrate "Nitrate")), [phosphorus](/wiki/Phosphorus "Phosphorus"), and [potassium](/wiki/Potassium "Potassium"), has affected the mouths of drainage basins. The minerals are carried by the drainage basin to the mouth, and may accumulate there, disturbing the natural mineral balance. This can cause [eutrophication](/wiki/Eutrophication "Eutrophication") where plant growth is accelerated by the additional material.

### Resource management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Resource management")]

Further information: [Watershed management](/wiki/Watershed_management "Watershed management")

Because drainage basins are coherent entities in a hydrological sense, it has become common to manage water resources on the basis of individual basins. In the [U.S. state of Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"), governmental entities that perform this function are called "[watershed districts](/wiki/Watershed_district_(Minnesota) "Watershed district (Minnesota)")".[[18]](#cite_note-18) In New Zealand, they are called catchment boards. Comparable community groups based in Ontario, Canada, are called [conservation authorities](/wiki/Conservation_authority_(Canada) "Conservation authority (Canada)"). In North America, this function is referred to as "[watershed management](/wiki/Watershed_management "Watershed management")".
In [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), the National Policy of Water Resources, regulated by Act n° 9.433 of 1997, establishes the drainage basin as the territorial division of Brazilian water management.

When a river basin crosses at least one political border, either a border within a nation or an international boundary, it is identified as a [transboundary river](/wiki/Transboundary_river "Transboundary river"). Management of such basins becomes the responsibility of the countries sharing it. [Nile Basin Initiative](/wiki/Nile_Basin_Initiative "Nile Basin Initiative"), [OMVS](/wiki/OMVS "OMVS") for [Senegal River](/wiki/Senegal_River "Senegal River"), [Mekong River Commission](/wiki/Mekong_River_Commission "Mekong River Commission") are a few examples of arrangements involving management of shared river basins.

Management of shared drainage basins is also seen as a way to build lasting peaceful relationships among countries.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Catchment factors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Catchment factors")]

The catchment is the most significant factor determining the amount or likelihood of [flooding](/wiki/Flooding "Flooding").

Catchment factors are: [topography](/wiki/Topography "Topography"), shape, size, [soil](/wiki/Soil "Soil") type, and [land use](/wiki/Land_use "Land use") (paved or [roofed](/wiki/Roof "Roof") areas). Catchment topography and shape determine the time taken for [rain](/wiki/Rain "Rain") to reach the river, while catchment size, soil type, and development determine the amount of water to reach the river.

### Topography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Topography")]

Generally, topography plays a big part in how fast runoff will reach a river. Rain that falls in steep [mountainous](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain") areas will reach the primary river in the drainage basin faster than flat or lightly sloping areas (e.g., > 1% gradient).

### Shape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Shape")]

Shape will contribute to the speed with which the runoff reaches a river. A long thin catchment will take longer to drain than a circular catchment.

### Size

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Size")]

Size will help determine the amount of water reaching the river, as the larger the catchment the greater the potential for flooding. It is also determined on the basis of length and width of the drainage basin.

### Soil type

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Soil type")]

Soil type will help determine how much water reaches the river. The runoff from the drainage area is dependent on the soil type. Certain soil types such as [sandy](/wiki/Sand "Sand") soils are very free-draining, and rainfall on sandy soil is likely to be absorbed by the ground. However, soils containing [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay") can be almost impermeable and therefore rainfall on clay soils will run off and contribute to flood volumes. After prolonged rainfall even free-draining soils can become [saturated](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/saturated "wikt:saturated"), meaning that any further rainfall will reach the river rather than being absorbed by the ground. If the surface is impermeable the precipitation will create surface run-off which will lead to higher risk of flooding; if the ground is permeable, the precipitation will infiltrate the soil.[[5]](#cite_note-Huneau-2013-5)

### Land use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drainage_basin&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Land use")]

Land use can contribute to the volume of water reaching the river, in a similar way to clay soils. For example, rainfall on roofs, [pavements](/wiki/Sidewalk "Sidewalk"), and [roads](/wiki/Road "Road") will be collected by rivers with almost no absorption into the [groundwater](/wiki/Groundwater "Groundwater").
A drainage basin is an area of land where all flowing surface water converges to a single point, such as a river mouth, or flows into another body of water, such as a lake or ocean.
